summary: Joe Reis observes that despite growing enthusiasm at data engineering conferences worldwide, the profession remains fragmented with practitioners repeatedly facing the same challenges in isolation. He argues that for data engineering to mature, the community needs to think globally and develop standardized practices rather than having every company solve the same problems from scratch.
key_points: Data engineering communities are enthusiastic but fragmented globally
Practitioners repeatedly face the same pain points without shared resources
Every company treats data engineering as a cold-start problem
The profession needs standardization to mature
Global thinking is required for data engineering to grow
money_quotes: It seems like every company embarks on data engineering on its own, and it's a cold-start problem every single time.
My big question is, if data engineering is to grow as a profession, why don't we have a way to help standardize our practices?
I've traveled the globe, and I have a good sense of where data engineering is on a macro level. We've got a lot of work to do.
For data engineering to mature as a profession, we'll need to start thinking globally.
key_insight: Data engineering's maturation as a profession is hindered by fragmentation, requiring a shift toward global community-building and standardized practices to prevent every organization from reinventing the wheel.
